title: Square API keys should not be disclosed
description: In most cases, trust boundaries are violated when a secret is exposed in a source code repository or an uncontrolled deployment environment. Unintended people who don’t need to know the secret might get access to it. They might then be able to use it to gain unwanted access to associated services or resources.
recommendation: Review and fix according to the rule guidelines.

checks.message: Square Access Token detected - CRITICAL. This token (sq0atp-xxx) provides programmatic access to Square payment processing APIs, allowing unauthorized parties to process transactions, access customer payment data, manage inventory, retrieve sales reports, and perform financial operations on merchant accounts. Exposed tokens enable attackers to: 1) Process fraudulent payments and refunds, 2) Access customer credit card information and PII (name, email, phone), 3) Manipulate inventory and pricing, 4) Exfiltrate sales and financial data, 5) Create payment links for phishing attacks, 6) Modify merchant settings and webhooks. IMMEDIATE ACTIONS: 1) Revoke the exposed access token immediately via Square Developer Dashboard (https://developer.squareup.com), 2) Generate new access token with restricted permissions, 3) Rotate OAuth credentials if applicable, 4) Review access logs and transaction history for suspicious activity (unusual payment amounts, new payment methods, location anomalies), 5) Store tokens securely using environment variables or secret management systems (AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault, Azure Key Vault), 6) Implement least-privilege access with Square API scopes (limit to only required permissions), 7) Enable webhook signature verification to prevent callback attacks, 8) Review and remove committed token from version control history (git-filter-branch, BFG Repo-Cleaner), 9) Audit OAuth applications with access to the account, 10) Enable two-factor authentication on Square account, 11) Implement API rate limiting and monitoring, 12) Consider using Square Web Payments SDK for frontend payments instead of exposing access tokens.

Square OAuth Secret detected - CRITICAL. This OAuth client secret (sq0csp-xxx) is used for authenticating OAuth applications that integrate with Square merchant accounts. Exposure allows attackers to: 1) Impersonate your OAuth application to obtain access tokens from merchant accounts, 2) Steal authorization codes during OAuth flows to gain unauthorized access, 3) Access all merchant accounts that have authorized your application, 4) Perform man-in-the-middle attacks on OAuth authorization, 5) Create fraudulent OAuth applications masquerading as your legitimate app, 6) Bypass application-level security controls. IMMEDIATE ACTIONS: 1) Revoke the exposed OAuth client secret immediately via Square Developer Dashboard application settings, 2) Generate new OAuth client secret and update all production instances, 3) Notify all merchants using your OAuth application about the security incident (compliance requirement), 4) Review OAuth authorization logs for unauthorized access or suspicious authorization patterns, 5) Audit all access tokens issued to ensure legitimacy, 6) Revoke suspicious access tokens, 7) Store OAuth secrets in secure vault systems (never in source code), 8) Implement OAuth state parameter validation to prevent CSRF attacks, 9) Use PKCE (Proof Key for Code Exchange) for enhanced security, 10) Review webhook endpoints for tampering, 11) Enable webhook signature verification, 12) Audit all Square applications in your developer account, 13) Check for unauthorized application registrations, 14) Implement strict redirect URI validation, 15) Enable fraud detection monitoring on Square dashboard.

Square Application ID detected - WARNING. This Application ID (sq0idb-xxx for production or sq0idp-xxx for sandbox) uniquely identifies your Square application. While less sensitive than access tokens or OAuth secrets, exposure can lead to: 1) Information disclosure about your Square integration architecture, 2) Reconnaissance for targeted attacks, 3) Identification of sandbox vs production environments, 4) Potential for application-level enumeration attacks, 5) Discovery of webhook endpoints associated with the application. RECOMMENDED ACTIONS: 1) While Application IDs are less sensitive, avoid committing them to public repositories, 2) Use environment variables for configuration management, 3) Implement application-level security controls (rate limiting, input validation, webhook signature verification), 4) Monitor Square Developer Dashboard for unauthorized application modifications, 5) Enable webhook signature validation using your webhook signature key, 6) Review application permissions and ensure least-privilege configuration, 7) Audit OAuth redirect URIs for unauthorized entries, 8) Enable two-factor authentication on Square Developer account, 9) Regularly review access logs for anomalies, 10) Consider rotating Application ID if exposure is suspected (requires updating all deployments).
